    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1124
    21→
    22→Can a square and a circle of the same area be decomposed into a finite number of
    23→congruent parts? (Tarski's circle-squaring problem.)
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1124](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1124)
    26→
    27→[La90b] Laczkovich, M., _Equidecomposability and discrepancy; a solution of Tarski's
    28→circle-squaring problem_. J. Reine Angew. Math. **404** (1990), 77-117.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos1124
    32→
    33→/-- The unit square $[0,1]^2$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    34→noncomputable def unitSquare : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) :=
    35→  {p | ∀ i, 0 ≤ p i ∧ p i ≤ 1}
    36→
    37→/--
    38→Erdős Problem 1124 (Tarski's circle-squaring problem, proved):
    39→
    40→Can a square and a circle of the same area be decomposed into a finite number
    41→of congruent parts?
    42→
    43→A problem of Tarski, which Erdős described as 'a very beautiful problem...if it
    44→were my problem I would offer \$1000 for it'.
    45→
    46→Laczkovich [La90b] proved that this is possible using translations only.
    47→
    48→Formally: the unit square and the closed disk of radius $1/\sqrt{\pi}$ (both having area $1$)
    49→can be partitioned into finitely many pieces such that corresponding pieces are
    50→congruent (related by isometries of $\mathbb{R}^2$).
    51→-/
    52→@[category research solved, AMS 28 51]
    53→theorem erdos_1124 : answer(True) ↔
    54→    ∃ (n : ℕ),
    55→    ∃ (pieces_sq pieces_disk : Fin n → Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    56→      -- The pieces partition the unit square
    57→      (⋃ i, pieces_sq i) = unitSquare ∧
    58→      (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Disjoint (pieces_sq i) (pieces_sq j)) ∧
    59→      -- The pieces partition the closed disk of radius 1/√π (same area as unit square)
    60→      (⋃ i, pieces_disk i) =
    61→        Metric.closedBall (0 : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (1 / Real.sqrt Real.pi) ∧
    62→      (∀ i j, i ≠ j → Disjoint (pieces_disk i) (pieces_disk j)) ∧
    63→      -- Corresponding pieces are congruent (related by an isometry)
    64→      (∀ i, ∃ f : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
    65→        Isometry f ∧ f '' (pieces_sq i) = pieces_disk i) := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos1124
    69→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 214
    21→
    22→Let $S \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be such that no two points in $S$ are distance $1$ apart.
    23→Must the complement of $S$ contain four points which form a unit square?
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/214](https://www.erdosproblems.com/214)
    26→
    27→[Ju79] Juhász, R., _Ramsey type theorems in the plane_, 1979.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→namespace Erdos214
    31→
    32→/--
    33→Four points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a "unit square" if, when taken in cyclic order
    34→$a \to b \to c \to d$, all four sides have length $1$ and both diagonals have length $\sqrt{2}$.
    35→The four points must also be pairwise distinct.
    36→-/
    37→def IsUnitSquare (a b c d : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : Prop :=
    38→  a ≠ b ∧ a ≠ c ∧ a ≠ d ∧ b ≠ c ∧ b ≠ d ∧ c ≠ d ∧
    39→  dist a b = 1 ∧ dist b c = 1 ∧ dist c d = 1 ∧ dist d a = 1 ∧
    40→  dist a c = Real.sqrt 2 ∧ dist b d = Real.sqrt 2
    41→
    42→/--
    43→Let $S \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be such that no two points in $S$ are distance $1$ apart.
    44→Then the complement of $S$ must contain four points which form a unit square.
    45→
    46→This was proved by Juhász [Ju79], who showed more generally that the complement of $S$
    47→must contain a congruent copy of any set of four points.
    48→-/
    49→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    50→theorem erdos_214 :
    51→    ∀ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    52→      (∀ p ∈ S, ∀ q ∈ S, dist p q ≠ 1) →
    53→      ∃ a b c d : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
    54→        a ∈ Sᶜ ∧ b ∈ Sᶜ ∧ c ∈ Sᶜ ∧ d ∈ Sᶜ ∧
    55→        IsUnitSquare a b c d := by
    56→  sorry
    57→
    58→end Erdos214
    59→

    14→limitations under the License.

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 106
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/106](https://www.erdosproblems.com/106)
    23→
    24→Draw $n$ non-overlapping squares (possibly rotated) inside the unit square. Let $f(n)$ be
    25→the maximum total side length. Is $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$?
    26→-/
    27→
    28→open BigOperators Real
    29→
    30→namespace Erdos106
    31→
    32→/--
    33→A square placement in $\mathbb{R}^2$: a center point, a positive side length, and a
    34→rotation angle (in radians) measuring how far the square is rotated from
    35→the standard axis-aligned orientation.
    36→-/
    37→structure SquarePlacement where
    38→  center : ℝ × ℝ
    39→  side   : ℝ
    40→  angle  : ℝ
    41→  side_pos : 0 < side
    42→
    43→/--
    44→The closed region occupied by a square placement. A point $p$ lies in the
    45→region iff, when translated so the center is at the origin and then rotated
    46→by $-\mathrm{angle}$, its coordinates both lie in $[-\mathrm{side}/2, \mathrm{side}/2]$.
    47→-/
    48→noncomputable def SquarePlacement.region (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    49→  {p : ℝ × ℝ |
    50→    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    51→    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    52→    |u| ≤ sq.side / 2 ∧ |v| ≤ sq.side / 2}
    53→
    54→/--
    55→The open interior of a square placement (strict inequalities).
    56→-/
    57→noncomputable def SquarePlacement.sqInterior (sq : SquarePlacement) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    58→  {p : ℝ × ℝ |
    59→    let u :=  (p.1 - sq.center.1) * cos sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * sin sq.angle
    60→    let v := -(p.1 - sq.center.1) * sin sq.angle + (p.2 - sq.center.2) * cos sq.angle
    61→    |u| < sq.side / 2 ∧ |v| < sq.side / 2}
    62→
    63→/--
    64→The unit square $[0,1]^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$.
    65→-/
    66→def unitSquare : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    67→  {p : ℝ × ℝ | 0 ≤ p.1 ∧ p.1 ≤ 1 ∧ 0 ≤ p.2 ∧ p.2 ≤ 1}
    68→
    69→/--
    70→A valid configuration of $n$ squares inside the unit square:
    71→1. Each square's closed region is contained in the unit square.
    72→2. No two distinct squares share an interior point (their open interiors
    73→   are disjoint).
    74→-/
    75→def IsValidSquareConfig (n : ℕ) (config : Fin n → SquarePlacement) : Prop :=
    76→  (∀ i : Fin n, (config i).region ⊆ unitSquare) ∧
    77→  (∀ i j : Fin n, i ≠ j → Disjoint (config i).sqInterior (config j).sqInterior)
    78→
    79→/--
    80→$f(n)$ is the supremum of the total side-length sum over all valid configurations
    81→of $n$ (possibly rotated) squares inside the unit square with pairwise disjoint
    82→interiors.
    83→-/
    84→noncomputable def f (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    85→  sSup {s : ℝ | ∃ config : Fin n → SquarePlacement,
    86→    IsValidSquareConfig n config ∧ s = ∑ i : Fin n, (config i).side}
    87→
    88→/--
    89→Erdős Problem 106:
    90→
    91→Draw $n$ squares (not necessarily axis-aligned) inside the unit square $[0,1]^2$
    92→with no two squares sharing a common interior point. Let $f(n)$ be the maximum
    93→possible sum of the side-lengths of the squares.
    94→
    95→**Conjecture**: $f(k^2 + 1) = k$ for every positive integer $k$.
    96→
    97→Background:
    98→- It follows easily from the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality that $f(k^2) = k$.
    99→- The lower bound $f(k^2 + 1) \geq k$ is elementary: subdivide $[0,1]^2$ into $k^2$
   100→  squares of side $1/k$, then replace any one of them by two squares of side
   101→  $1/(2k)$; the total side-length is $(k^2 - 1)/k + 2 \cdot 1/(2k) = k$.
   102→- The conjecture asserts that this lower bound is tight: no configuration of
   103→  $k^2 + 1$ squares can exceed the total side-length $k$ achievable with $k^2$ squares.
   104→- Baek, Koizumi, and Ueoro (2024) proved the axis-aligned variant: if all
   105→  squares are required to have sides parallel to the coordinate axes, then the
   106→  supremum equals $k$.
   107→-/
   108→@[category research open, AMS 52]
   109→theorem erdos_106 :
   110→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k : ℕ, 0 < k → f (k ^ 2 + 1) = (k : ℝ) := by
   111→  sorry
   112→
   113→end Erdos106
   114→

    27→open Set Metric EuclideanGeometry Order

    30→
    31→/-- Two segments are disjoint if they only intersect at their endpoints (if at all). -/
    32→def SegmentsDisjoint (seg1 seg2 : ℝ² × ℝ²) : Prop :=
    33→  segment ℝ seg1.1 seg1.2 ∩ segment ℝ seg2.1 seg2.2 ⊆ {seg1.1, seg1.2, seg2.1, seg2.2}
    34→
    35→/--
    36→Can a finite set of disjoint unit segments in a unit square be maximal?
    37→Solved affirmatively by [Da85], who gave an explicit construction.
    38→
    39→This was formalized in Lean by Alexeev using Aristotle and ChatGPT.
    40→-/
    41→@[category research formally solved using lean4 at
    42→"https://github.com/plby/lean-proofs/blob/main/src/v4.24.0/ErdosProblems/Erdos1071.lean", AMS 52]
    43→theorem erdos_1071.parts.i :
    44→    answer(True) ↔ ∃ S : Finset (ℝ² × ℝ²),
    45→      Maximal (fun T : Finset (ℝ² × ℝ²) =>
    46→        (∀ seg ∈ T, dist seg.1 seg.2 = 1 ∧
    47→          seg.1 0 ∈ Icc 0 1 ∧ seg.1 1 ∈ Icc 0 1 ∧
    48→          seg.2 0 ∈ Icc 0 1 ∧ seg.2 1 ∈ Icc 0 1) ∧
    49→          (T : Set (ℝ² × ℝ²)).Pairwise SegmentsDisjoint) S := by
    50→  sorry
    51→
    52→/-- Is there a region $R$ with a maximal set of disjoint unit line segments that is countably infinite? -/
    53→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    54→theorem erdos_1071.parts.ii :
    55→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∃ (R : Set ℝ²) (S : Set (ℝ² × ℝ²)),
    56→      S.Countable ∧ S.Infinite ∧
    57→      Maximal (fun T : Set (ℝ² × ℝ²) =>
    58→        (∀ seg ∈ T, dist seg.1 seg.2 = 1 ∧ seg.1 ∈ R ∧ seg.2 ∈ R) ∧
    59→        T.Pairwise SegmentsDisjoint) S := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→end Erdos1071
    63→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1159
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1159](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1159)
    23→
    24→Determine whether there exists a constant $C > 1$ such that every finite projective plane
    25→has a blocking set where no line is hit more than $C$ times. A blocking set is a set of
    26→points that meets every line.
    27→
    28→Erdős, Silverman, and Stein [ESS83] proved this is true with
    29→$|S \cap \ell| \ll \log n$ for all lines $\ell$ (where $n$ is the order of the projective
    30→plane).
    31→
    32→[ESS83] Erdős, P., Silverman, R., and Stein, A.
    33→
    34→[Va99] Vardy, A.

    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 1159 [Va99, 4.70]:
    43→
    44→Does there exist an absolute constant $C > 1$ such that every finite projective plane
    45→has a set of points $S$ meeting every line in at least $1$ and at most $C$ points?
    46→-/

    50→      ∀ (P L : Type) [Membership P L] [Fintype P] [Fintype L]
    51→        [Configuration.ProjectivePlane P L],
    52→        ∃ S : Finset P,
    53→          ∀ l : L, 1 ≤ (S.filter (fun p => p ∈ l)).card ∧
    54→                    (S.filter (fun p => p ∈ l)).card ≤ C := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos1159

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1090
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1090](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1090)
    23→
    24→Let $k \geq 3$. Does there exist a finite set $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ such that, in any
    25→2-colouring of $A$, there exists a line which contains at least $k$ points from $A$, and all the
    26→points of $A$ on the line have the same colour?
    27→
    28→[Er75f] Erdős, P., *Problems and results on combinatorial number theory III*.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos1090
    32→
    33→/--
    34→Let $k \geq 3$. Does there exist a finite set $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ such that, in any
    35→2-colouring of $A$, there exists a line which contains at least $k$ points from $A$, and all the
    36→points of $A$ on the line have the same colour?
    37→
    38→The answer is yes. Erdős [Er75f] notes that Graham and Selfridge proved the
    39→case $k = 3$. Hunter observed that for sufficiently large $n$, a generic projection
    40→of $[k]^n$ into $\mathbb{R}^2$ has this property, by the Hales–Jewett theorem.
    41→-/
    42→@[category research solved, AMS 5 51]
    43→theorem erdos_1090 : answer(True) ↔ ∀ (k : ℕ), k ≥ 3 →
    44→    ∃ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    45→      ∀ c : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) → Fin 2,
    46→        ∃ L : AffineSubspace ℝ (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    47→          Module.finrank ℝ L.direction = 1 ∧
    48→          k ≤ Set.ncard {p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) | p ∈ (A : Set _) ∧ p ∈ L} ∧
    49→          ∃ color : Fin 2, ∀ p ∈ (A : Set _), p ∈ L → c p = color := by
    50→  sorry
    51→
    52→end Erdos1090

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1002
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1002](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1002)
    23→
    24→For any $0 < \alpha < 1$, let
    25→$$f(\alpha, n) = \frac{1}{\log n} \sum_{1 \le k \le n} \left(\frac{1}{2} - \{\alpha k\}\right)$$
    26→where $\{\cdot\}$ denotes the fractional part.
    27→
    28→Does $f(\alpha, n)$ have an asymptotic distribution function? In other words, is there
    29→a non-decreasing function $g$ such that $g(-\infty) = 0$, $g(\infty) = 1$, and
    30→$$\lim_{n \to \infty} |\{\alpha \in (0,1) : f(\alpha,n) \le c\}| = g(c)$$
    31→where $|\cdot|$ denotes Lebesgue measure?
    32→
    33→Kesten [Ke60] proved the analogous result with an additional shift $\beta$:
    34→if $f(\alpha, \beta, n) = \frac{1}{\log n} \sum_{k=1}^n \left(\frac{1}{2} - \{\beta + \alpha k\}\right)$,
    35→then $f(\alpha, \beta, n)$ has asymptotic distribution function
    36→$g(c) = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\rho c} \frac{1}{1+t^2} \, dt$
    37→where $\rho > 0$ is an explicit constant.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open Finset Filter MeasureTheory
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos1002
    43→
    44→/-- The function $f(\alpha, n) = \frac{1}{\log n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \left(\frac{1}{2} - \{\alpha k\}\right)$,
    45→where $\{\cdot\}$ denotes the fractional part. -/
    46→noncomputable def erdosF (α : ℝ) (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    47→  (∑ k ∈ Finset.Icc 1 n, ((1 : ℝ) / 2 - Int.fract (α * ↑k))) / Real.log ↑n
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 1002 [Er64b]:
    51→
    52→Does the function $f(\alpha, n) = \frac{1}{\log n} \sum_{k=1}^n \left(\frac{1}{2} - \{\alpha k\}\right)$
    53→have an asymptotic distribution function? That is, is there a non-decreasing function
    54→$g : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ with $g(-\infty) = 0$ and $g(\infty) = 1$ such that for every
    55→$c \in \mathbb{R}$,
    56→$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \mu(\{\alpha \in (0,1) : f(\alpha,n) \le c\}) = g(c)$$
    57→where $\mu$ is Lebesgue measure.
    58→-/
    59→@[category research open, AMS 11 28]
    60→theorem erdos_1002 : answer(sorry) ↔
    61→    ∃ g : ℝ → ℝ, Monotone g ∧
    62→    Filter.Tendsto g Filter.atBot (nhds 0) ∧
    63→    Filter.Tendsto g Filter.atTop (nhds 1) ∧
    64→    ∀ c : ℝ, Filter.Tendsto
    65→      (fun n : ℕ =>
    66→        (volume (Set.Ioo (0 : ℝ) 1 ∩ {α : ℝ | erdosF α n ≤ c})).toReal)
    67→      Filter.atTop (nhds (g c)) := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→end Erdos1002
    71→

    20→# Erdős Problem 1154

    23→
    24→Does there exist, for every α ∈ [0,1], a subring of ℝ with Hausdorff dimension α?

    34→Erdős Problem #1154 [Er79h, p.119]:
    35→
    36→Does there exist, for every $\alpha \in [0,1]$, a subring of $\mathbb{R}$ with Hausdorff
    37→dimension $\alpha$?
    38→
    39→Erdős and Volkmann proved the analogous result for subgroups of $\mathbb{R}$.
    40→Falconer showed that any subring with Hausdorff dimension $\alpha \in (1/2,1)$ cannot be
    41→Borel or Suslin. Edgar and Miller proved that any Borel or analytic subring of $\mathbb{R}$
    42→either has Hausdorff dimension $0$ or equals $\mathbb{R}$. Mauldin proved the result for
    43→subfields assuming the continuum hypothesis.

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1007
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1007](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1007)
    23→
    24→The dimension of a graph $G$ is the minimal $n$ such that $G$ can be embedded in
    25→$\mathbb{R}^n$ such that every edge of $G$ is a unit line segment (adjacent vertices are at
    26→Euclidean distance exactly $1$, and non-adjacent vertices are not). This notion
    27→was defined by Erdős, Harary, and Tutte.
    28→
    29→[So09e] Soifer, A., *The Mathematical Coloring Book*, Springer, 2009.
    30→
    31→[Ho13] House, J., *Graphs of dimension 4*, 2013.
    32→
    33→[ChNo16] Chaffee, J. and Noble, S., *An alternative proof*, 2016.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open SimpleGraph
    37→
    38→namespace Erdos1007
    39→
    40→/-- A unit-distance representation of a simple graph $G$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$: an injective map
    41→from vertices to Euclidean $n$-space such that adjacency is equivalent to
    42→distance exactly $1$. -/
    43→def IsUnitDistRep {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (n : ℕ)
    44→    (f : V → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    45→  Function.Injective f ∧
    46→  ∀ u v, G.Adj u v ↔ dist (f u) (f v) = 1
    47→
    48→/-- A graph admits a unit-distance representation in $\mathbb{R}^n$. -/
    49→def HasUnitDistRep {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    50→  ∃ f : V → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n), IsUnitDistRep G n f
    51→
    52→/--
    53→Erdős Problem 1007 [So09e]:
    54→
    55→The smallest number of edges in a graph of dimension exactly $4$ is $9$, where the
    56→dimension of a graph is the minimal $n$ for a unit-distance representation in $\mathbb{R}^n$.
    57→
    58→This is achieved uniquely by $K_{3,3}$. Proved by House [Ho13], with an alternative
    59→proof by Chaffee and Noble [ChNo16].
    60→-/
    61→@[category research solved, AMS 5 51]
    62→theorem erdos_1007 :
    63→    -- There exists a graph of dimension exactly 4 with 9 edges
    64→    (∃ (V : Type) (_ : Fintype V) (_ : DecidableEq V) (G : SimpleGraph V)
    65→       (_ : DecidableRel G.Adj),
    66→       HasUnitDistRep G 4 ∧ ¬HasUnitDistRep G 3 ∧ G.edgeFinset.card = 9) ∧
    67→    -- Every graph of dimension exactly 4 has at least 9 edges
    68→    (∀ (V : Type) [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V] (G : SimpleGraph V)
    69→       [DecidableRel G.Adj],
    70→       HasUnitDistRep G 4 → ¬HasUnitDistRep G 3 → 9 ≤ G.edgeFinset.card) := by
    71→  sorry
    72→
    73→end Erdos1007
    74→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1001
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1001](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1001)
    23→
    24→Let $S(N, A, c)$ be the Lebesgue measure of the set of $\alpha \in (0,1)$ such that
    25→$|\alpha - x/y| < A/y^2$
    26→for some $N \leq y \leq cN$ and $\gcd(x,y) = 1$. Does
    27→$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N, A, c) = f(A, c)$
    28→exist? What is its explicit form?
    29→
    30→A problem of Erdős, Szüsz, and Turán [EST58], who proved the formula
    31→$f(A, c) = 12A \log c / \pi^2$
    32→when $0 < A < c/(1 + c^2)$.
    33→
    34→The existence of the limit was proved by Kesten and Sós [KeSo66].
    35→Alternative, more explicit proofs were given by Boca [Bo08] and
    36→Xiong–Zaharescu [XiZa06].
    37→
    38→[Er64b] Erdős, P.
    39→
    40→[EST58] Erdős, P., Szüsz, P., and Turán, P.
    41→
    42→[KeSo66] Kesten, H. and Sós, V. T.
    43→
    44→[Bo08] Boca, F. P.
    45→
    46→[XiZa06] Xiong, M. and Zaharescu, A.
    47→-/
    48→
    49→open MeasureTheory Set Filter
    50→
    51→namespace Erdos1001
    52→
    53→/-- The set of $\alpha \in (0,1)$ approximable by a coprime fraction $x/y$
    54→    with $N \leq y \leq cN$ to within $A/y^2$. -/
    55→noncomputable def approxSet (N : ℕ) (A c : ℝ) : Set ℝ :=
    56→  {α : ℝ | α ∈ Ioo 0 1 ∧
    57→    ∃ (x : ℤ) (y : ℕ), N ≤ y ∧ (y : ℝ) ≤ c * N ∧
    58→      Nat.Coprime (Int.natAbs x) y ∧
    59→      |α - (x : ℝ) / (y : ℝ)| < A / ((y : ℝ) ^ 2)}
    60→
    61→/-- $S(N, A, c)$ is the Lebesgue measure of the approximation set. -/
    62→noncomputable def sMeasure (N : ℕ) (A c : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    63→  (volume (approxSet N A c)).toReal
    64→
    65→/--
    66→Erdős Problem 1001 [Er64b]:
    67→
    68→For all $A > 0$ and $c > 1$, the limit $\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N, A, c)$ exists.
    69→
    70→Proved by Kesten and Sós [KeSo66].
    71→-/
    72→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    73→theorem erdos_1001 : answer(True) ↔
    74→    ∀ (A c : ℝ), 0 < A → 1 < c →
    75→      ∃ L : ℝ, Tendsto (fun N : ℕ => sMeasure N A c) atTop (nhds L) := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos1001
    79→

    20→# Erdős Problem 910
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/910](https://www.erdosproblems.com/910)
    23→
    24→Does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ contain a connected subset which is not a point
    25→and not homeomorphic to the original set? If $n \geq 2$, does every connected set in
    26→$\mathbb{R}^n$ contain more than $2^{\aleph_0}$ many connected subsets?
    27→
    28→Asked by Erdős in the 1940s, who thought the answer to both questions is yes.
    29→The answer to both is in fact no, as shown by Rudin [Ru58] (conditional on the
    30→continuum hypothesis).

    43→point and not homeomorphic to the original set?

    47→    ∀ (n : ℕ), n ≥ 1 →
    48→    ∀ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    49→    IsConnected S →
    50→    ∃ (T : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin n))),
    51→      T ⊆ S ∧ IsConnected T ∧ T.Nontrivial ∧
    52→      IsEmpty (Homeomorph T S) := by
    53→  sorry

    55→/--
    56→Erdős Problem 910, Part 2 (disproved by Rudin [Ru58]):
    57→
    58→If $n \geq 2$, does every connected set in $\mathbb{R}^n$ have more than $2^{\aleph_0}$ many
    59→connected subsets?

    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 215
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/215](https://www.erdosproblems.com/215)
    23→
    24→Does there exist a set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that every set congruent to $S$
    25→contains exactly one point of $\mathbb{Z}^2$? The answer is yes, proved by Jackson and
    26→Mauldin [JaMa02] using the axiom of choice.
    27→
    28→[JaMa02] Jackson, S. and Mauldin, R.D., _On a lattice problem of H. Steinhaus_, Journal of the
    29→American Mathematical Society 15 (2002), 817-856.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos215
    33→
    34→/--
    35→The integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^2 \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$: points whose coordinates are all
    36→integers.
    37→-/
    38→def IntLattice : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) :=
    39→  {p | ∀ i : Fin 2, ∃ n : ℤ, p i = ↑n}
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 215 (Steinhaus Problem):
    43→Does there exist $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ such that every set congruent to $S$
    44→(that is, $S$ after some isometry) contains exactly one point from $\mathbb{Z}^2$?
    45→
    46→The answer is yes, proved by Jackson and Mauldin [JaMa02].
    47→Their construction depends on the axiom of choice.
    48→-/
    49→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    50→theorem erdos_215 : answer(True) ↔
    51→    ∃ (S : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    52→      ∀ (f : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    53→        Isometry f →
    54→        ∃! p, p ∈ f '' S ∧ p ∈ IntLattice := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos215
    58→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 501
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/501](https://www.erdosproblems.com/501)
    23→
    24→For every $x \in \mathbb{R}$, let $A_x \subset \mathbb{R}$ be a bounded set with outer measure
    25→$< 1$. Must there exist an infinite independent set, that is, some infinite $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$
    26→such that $x \notin A_y$ for all $x \neq y \in X$?
    27→
    28→If the sets $A_x$ are closed and have measure $< 1$, then must there exist an
    29→independent set of size $3$?
    30→
    31→Erdős and Hajnal proved the existence of arbitrarily large finite independent
    32→sets (under the assumptions in the first problem). Hechler showed the answer
    33→to the first question is no, assuming the continuum hypothesis. Newelski,
    34→Pawlikowski, and Seredyński proved that if all the $A_x$ are closed with
    35→measure $< 1$ then there is an infinite independent set.
    36→
    37→[Er61] Erdős, P., _Problems in combinatorics_ (1961).
    38→
    39→[ErHa71] Erdős, P. and Hajnal, A., _On a property of families of sets_ (1971).
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open MeasureTheory Set
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos501
    45→
    46→/-- A set $X$ is independent with respect to a family of sets $A$ if for all
    47→distinct $x, y \in X$, we have $x \notin A(y)$. -/
    48→def IndependentFamily (A : ℝ → Set ℝ) (X : Set ℝ) : Prop :=
    49→  ∀ x ∈ X, ∀ y ∈ X, x ≠ y → x ∉ A y
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 501 [Er61][ErHa71]: If each $A_x$ is bounded with outer measure $< 1$, must
    53→there exist an infinite independent set?
    54→
    55→Hechler showed the answer is no assuming the continuum hypothesis. The full
    56→ZFC status remains open.
    57→-/
    58→@[category research open, AMS 3 28]
    59→theorem erdos_501 :
    60→    answer(sorry) ↔
    61→    ∀ A : ℝ → Set ℝ,
    62→      (∀ x : ℝ, Bornology.IsBounded (A x) ∧ volume (A x) < 1) →
    63→      ∃ X : Set ℝ, X.Infinite ∧ IndependentFamily A X := by
    64→  sorry
    65→
    66→/--
    67→Erdős Problem 501, closed variant [Er61][ErHa71]: If each $A_x$ is closed with
    68→measure $< 1$, must there exist an independent set of size $3$?
    69→
    70→Newelski, Pawlikowski, and Seredyński proved that under these hypotheses there
    71→is in fact an infinite independent set.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research solved, AMS 3 28]
    74→theorem erdos_501.variants.closed_measure :
    75→    answer(True) ↔
    76→    ∀ A : ℝ → Set ℝ,
    77→      (∀ x : ℝ, IsClosed (A x) ∧ volume (A x) < 1) →
    78→      ∃ x y z : ℝ, x ≠ y ∧ y ≠ z ∧ x ≠ z ∧
    79→        IndependentFamily A {x, y, z} := by
    80→  sorry
    81→
    82→end Erdos501

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 492
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/492](https://www.erdosproblems.com/492)
    23→
    24→Let $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots\} \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be infinite with $a_{i+1}/a_i \to 1$.
    25→The problem asked whether, for almost all $\alpha$, the sequence of fractional positions
    26→$f(\alpha n)$ is uniformly distributed in $[0,1)$. Disproved by Schmidt.
    27→
    28→[Sc69] Schmidt, W.M., _Irregularities of distribution. IV_, Invent. Math. 7 (1969), 55-82.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open Filter MeasureTheory Finset
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos492
    34→
    35→attribute [local instance] Classical.propDecidable
    36→
    37→/--
    38→A sequence $x : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ is equidistributed in $[0,1)$ if for every subinterval
    39→$[c, d) \subseteq [0,1)$, the proportion of indices $n < N$ with $x(n) \in [c, d)$
    40→converges to $d - c$ as $N \to \infty$.
    41→-/
    42→def IsEquidistributed (x : ℕ → ℝ) : Prop :=
    43→  ∀ c d : ℝ, 0 ≤ c → c < d → d ≤ 1 →
    44→    Tendsto (fun N : ℕ =>
    45→      (((Finset.range N).filter (fun n => c ≤ x n ∧ x n < d)).card : ℝ) / (N : ℝ))
    46→      atTop (nhds (d - c))
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Given a strictly increasing sequence $a : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$, for $x$ in some interval
    50→$[a(i), a(i+1))$, returns the fractional position
    51→$(x - a(i)) / (a(i+1) - a(i)) \in [0,1)$.
    52→Returns $0$ if $x$ is not in any such interval.
    53→-/
    54→noncomputable def fractionalPosition (a : ℕ → ℕ) (x : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    55→  if h : ∃ i : ℕ, (a i : ℝ) ≤ x ∧ x < (a (i + 1) : ℝ) then
    56→    let i := Nat.find h
    57→    (x - (a i : ℝ)) / ((a (i + 1) : ℝ) - (a i : ℝ))
    58→  else 0
    59→
    60→/--
    61→Erdős Problem 492 (disproved by Schmidt [Sc69]):
    62→
    63→Let $A = \{a_1 < a_2 < \cdots\} \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be infinite with $a_{i+1}/a_i \to 1$.
    64→For $x \geq a_1$, define $f(x) = (x - a_i)/(a_{i+1} - a_i) \in [0,1)$
    65→where $a_i \leq x < a_{i+1}$. For example if $A = \mathbb{N}$ then $f(x) = \{x\}$ is
    66→the usual fractional part operator.
    67→
    68→The conjecture asked whether for almost all $\alpha$, the sequence
    69→$f(\alpha n)$ is uniformly distributed in $[0,1)$. Schmidt showed this is false:
    70→there exists such a sequence for which equidistribution fails on a
    71→set of positive measure.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research solved, AMS 11 28]
    74→theorem erdos_492 : answer(False) ↔
    75→    ∀ (a : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono a → (∀ i, 0 < a i) →
    76→      Tendsto (fun i => (a (i + 1) : ℝ) / (a i : ℝ)) atTop (nhds 1) →
    77→      ∀ᵐ α ∂(volume : Measure ℝ),
    78→        IsEquidistributed (fun n => fractionalPosition a (α * (n : ℝ))) := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→end Erdos492
    82→

    24→
    25→scoped[EuclideanGeometry] notation "ℝ²" => EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    26→
    27→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    28→
    29→/-- Oriented angles make sense in 2d.
    30→
    31→Note: this can't blindly be added to mathlib as it creates an "instance diamond"
    32→with an instance for modules satisfying `is_empty`. -/
    33→noncomputable instance Module.orientedEuclideanSpaceFinTwo : Module.Oriented ℝ ℝ² (Fin 2) :=
    34→  ⟨Basis.orientation <| PiLp.basisFun 2 _ _⟩
    35→
    36→/-- Two dimensional euclidean space is two-dimensional. -/
    37→instance fact_finrank_euclideanSpace_fin_two : Fact (Module.finrank ℝ ℝ² = 2) :=
    38→  ⟨finrank_euclideanSpace_fin⟩

    40→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    41→
    42→open scoped Real
    43→
    44→namespace EuclideanGeometry
    45→
    46→variable {V P : Type*} {n : ℕ}
    47→
    48→variable [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V] [MetricSpace P] [NormedAddTorsor V P]
    49→
    50→variable [Module.Oriented ℝ V (Fin 2)] [Fact (Module.finrank ℝ V = 2)] {p : Fin n → P}
    51→
    52→/-- We say a subset `A` of points in the plane is non-trilinear
    53→if it contains no three points that lie on the same line. -/
    54→def NonTrilinear (A : Set P) : Prop :=
    55→  A.Triplewise (fun x y z ↦ ¬ Collinear ℝ {x, y, z})
    56→
    57→/-- `ConvexIndep S` means that `S` consists of extremal points of its convex hull,
    58→i.e., the point set encloses a convex shape.
    59→Also known as a "convex-independent set". -/
    60→def ConvexIndep (S : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    61→  ∀ a ∈ S, a ∉ convexHull ℝ (S \ {a})
    62→
    63→/-- The set `P` contains a convex `n`-gon.
    64→See also `IsConvexPolygon`. -/
    65→def HasConvexNGon (n : ℕ) (P : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    66→  ∃ S : Finset ℝ², S.card = n ∧ ↑S ⊆ P ∧ ConvexIndep S
    67→
    68→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a counter-clockwise convex polygon. -/
    69→def IsCcwConvexPolygon (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
    70→  ∀ ⦃i j k⦄, i < j → j < k → (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1
    71→
    72→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    73→  (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1 := hp hij hjk
    74→
    75→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    76→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    77→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p j) (p k) (p i)).sign = 1 := by
    78→  rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    79→  exact hp hij hjk
    80→
    81→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    82→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle'' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    83→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p k) (p i) (p j)).sign = 1 := by
    84→  rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    85→  exact hp hij hjk
    86→
    87→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle_finRotate (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p)
    88→    (hn : 3 ≤ n) (i : Fin n) :
    89→    (∡ (p i) (p <| finRotate _ i) (p <| finRotate _ (finRotate _ i))).sign = 1 := by
    90→  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := le_iff_exists_add'.mp hn
    91→  by_cases hi : i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    92→  · rw [hi, finRotate_last, finRotate_apply_zero]
    93→    exact hp.sign_oangle'' Fin.zero_lt_one Fin.one_lt_last
    94→  by_cases hi' : finRotate _ i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    95→  · rw [hi', finRotate_last]
    96→    refine hp.sign_oangle' ?_ ((Fin.le_last _).lt_of_ne hi)
    97→    rw [Fin.pos_iff_ne_zero]
    98→    rintro rfl
    99→    rw [finRotate_apply_zero] at hi'
   100→    exact Fin.one_lt_last.ne hi'
   101→  apply hp.sign_oangle <;> apply lt_finRotate_of_ne_last <;> assumption
   102→
   103→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_zero (p : Fin 0 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := finZeroElim
   104→
   105→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_one (p : Fin 1 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   106→
   107→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_two (p : Fin 2 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   108→
   109→set_option linter.docPrime false in
   110→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four' {p : Fin 4 → P} :
   111→    IsCcwConvexPolygon p ↔ (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 1) (p 2) (p 3)).sign = 1 ∧
   112→    (∡ (p 2) (p 3) (p 0)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 3) (p 0) (p 1)).sign = 1 := by
   113→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨h1, h2, h3, h4⟩ ↦ ?_⟩
   114→  · obtain ⟨h01, h12, h23⟩ : (0 : Fin 4) < 1 ∧ (1 : Fin 4) < 2 ∧ (2 : Fin 4) < 3 := by simp
   115→    · repeat' constructor
   116→      · exact h.sign_oangle h01 h12
   117→      · exact h.sign_oangle h12 h23
   118→      · exact h.sign_oangle' (h01.trans h12) h23
   119→      · exact h.sign_oangle'' h01 (h12.trans h23)
   120→  · intro i j k hij hjk
   121→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   122→    · exact h1
   123→    · rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   124→      exact h4
   125→    · rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   126→      exact h3
   127→    · exact h2
   128→
   129→@[simp]
   130→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four (A B C D : P) :
   131→    IsCcwConvexPolygon ![A, B, C, D] ↔
   132→      (∡ A B C).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ B C D).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ C D A).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ D A B).sign = 1 :=
   133→  isCcwConvexPolygon_four'
   134→
   135→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a convex polygon. -/
   136→def IsConvexPolygon {n : ℕ} (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
   137→  IsCcwConvexPolygon p ∨ IsCcwConvexPolygon fun i => p (-i)
   138→
   139→/-- Three affine independent points always form a convex polygon. -/
   140→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent {A B C : P}
   141→    (hABC : AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C]) : IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] := by
   142→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff] at hABC
   143→  cases hsABC : (∡ A B C).sign
   144→  · exact (hABC hsABC).elim
   145→  · right
   146→    intro i j k hij hjk
   147→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   148→    rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rev, Real.Angle.sign_neg, neg_eq_iff_eq_neg]
   149→    exact (oangle_rotate_sign A B C).trans hsABC
   150→  · left
   151→    intro i j k hij hjk
   152→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   153→    exact hsABC
   154→
   155→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent {A B C : P} :
   156→    IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] ↔ AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C] := by
   157→  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent⟩
   158→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff]
   159→  let p := ![A, B, C]
   160→  change IsConvexPolygon p at h
   161→  change Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)) ≠ 0
   162→  cases h with
   163→  | inl h =>
   164→    rw [h.sign_oangle (by simp) (by simp)]
   165→    rintro ⟨⟩
   166→  | inr h =>
   167→    suffices Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 2) (p 1)) = 1 by rw [← oangle_swap₂₃_sign, this]; rintro ⟨⟩
   168→    exact h.sign_oangle (i := 0) (j := 1) (k := 2) (by simp) (by simp)
   169→
   170→theorem isConvexPolygon_triangle (t : Affine.Triangle ℝ P) : IsConvexPolygon t.points := by
   171→  have : t.points = ![t.points 0, t.points 1, t.points 2] := by ext i; fin_cases i <;> rfl
   172→  rw [this, isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent, ← this]
   173→  exact t.independent
   174→
   175→noncomputable def triangle_area (a b c : P) : ℝ :=
   176→  positiveOrientation.areaForm (a -ᵥ c) (b -ᵥ c) / 2
   177→
   178→lemma triangle_area_eq_det (a b c : ℝ²) :
   179→    triangle_area a b c =
   180→    Matrix.det !![a 0, b 0, c 0;
   181→                  a 1, b 1, c 1;
   182→                  1,   1,   1] / 2 := by
   183→  rw [triangle_area, Orientation.areaForm_to_volumeForm,
   184→    positiveOrientation.volumeForm_robust (EuclideanSpace.basisFun (Fin 2) ℝ) rfl, Module.Basis.det_apply]
   185→  suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
   186→      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
   187→    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   188→  ring
   189→
   190→/--
   191→Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   192→of points.
   193→-/
   194→noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset ℝ²) : ℕ :=
   195→  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : ℝ² × ℝ²) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   196→
   197→end EuclideanGeometry
   198→
   199→
   200→def IsIsosceles {α : Type*} [Dist α] (p q r : α) : Prop :=
   201→  dist p q = dist q r ∨ dist q r = dist r p ∨ dist r p = dist p q
   202→
   203→nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {α : Type} [Dist α] (A : Set α) :=
   204→  Nonempty A ∧ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles · · ·)
   205→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 1038
    22→
    23→*Reference:*
    24→ - [erdosproblems.com/1038](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1038)
    25→ - [Tao25] Tao, Terence. Sublevel Sets of Logarithmic Potentials. Terry Tao’s Blog, Dec. 2025

    30→open MeasureTheory

    32→namespace Erdos1038
    33→
    34→/-- What is the infimum of `|{x ∈ ℝ : |f x| < 1}|` over all nonconstant monic polynomials `f` such
    35→that all of its roots are real and contained in `[-1,1]`? -/
    36→@[category research open, AMS 28]
    37→theorem erdos_1038.parts.i (n : ℕ) : answer(sorry) =
    38→    ⨅ f : {f : Polynomial ℝ // f.Monic ∧ f ≠ 1 ∧
    39→    (f.roots.filter fun x => x ∈ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1).card = f.natDegree},
    40→    volume {x | |f.1.eval x| < 1} := by
    41→  sorry
    42→
    43→/-- The supremum of `|{x ∈ ℝ : |f x| < 1}|` over all monic polynomials `f` such that
    44→all of its roots are real and contained in `[-1,1]` is `2 * 2 ^ (1 / 2)`. This is proved in
    45→[Tao25]. -/
    46→@[category research solved, AMS 28]
    47→theorem erdos_1038.parts.ii (n : ℕ) : 2 * 2 ^ (1 / 2 : ℝ) =
    48→    ⨆ f : {f : Polynomial ℝ // f.Monic ∧
    49→    (f.roots.filter fun x => x ∈ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1).card = f.natDegree},
    50→    volume {x | |f.1.eval x| < 1} := by
    51→  sorry

    53→/-- The infimum of `|{x ∈ ℝ : |f x| < 1}|` over all nonconstant monic polynomials `f` such that
    54→all of its roots are real and contained in `[-1,1]` is `< 1.835`. -/
    55→@[category research solved, AMS 28]
    56→theorem erdos_1038.variants.inf_upperBound (n : ℕ) : ⨅ f : {f : Polynomial ℝ // f.Monic ∧ f ≠ 1 ∧
    57→    (f.roots.filter fun x => x ∈ Set.Icc (-1 : ℝ) 1).card = f.natDegree},

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 232
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/232](https://www.erdosproblems.com/232)
    23→
    24→For $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ we define the upper density as
    25→$$\bar{\delta}(A) = \limsup_{R\to\infty} \frac{\lambda(A \cap B_R)}{\lambda(B_R)},$$
    26→where $\lambda$ is the Lebesgue measure and $B_R$ is the ball of radius $R$.
    27→
    28→Estimate $m_1 = \sup \bar{\delta}(A)$, where $A$ ranges over all measurable subsets of
    29→$\mathbb{R}^2$ without two points distance $1$ apart. In particular, is $m_1 \le 1/4$?
    30→
    31→Originally a question of Moser [Mo66]. The trivial upper bound is $m_1 \le 1/2$,
    32→since for any unit vector $u$ the sets $A$ and $A + u$ must be disjoint.
    33→A lower bound of $m_1 \ge \pi/(8\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.2267$ comes from the union of open
    34→circular discs of radius $1/2$ at a regular hexagonal lattice.
    35→
    36→Proved by Ambrus, Csiszárik, Matolcsi, Varga, and Zsámboki [ACMVZ23],
    37→who showed $m_1 \le 0.247 < 1/4$.
    38→
    39→[Mo66] Moser, L., *Problem 10*, Canad. Math. Bull. 9 (1966).
    40→
    41→[Er85] Erdős, P., *Problems and results in combinatorial geometry*, 1985, p. 4.
    42→
    43→[ACMVZ23] Ambrus, G., Csiszárik, A., Matolcsi, M., Varga, D., and Zsámboki, P.,
    44→*The density of planar sets avoiding unit distances*, 2023.
    45→-/
    46→
    47→open MeasureTheory Metric
    48→
    49→namespace Erdos232
    50→
    51→/-- A set in a metric space is *unit-distance-free* if no two points in the set
    52→are exactly distance $1$ apart. -/
    53→def IsUnitDistanceFree {X : Type*} [PseudoMetricSpace X] (A : Set X) : Prop :=
    54→  ∀ x ∈ A, ∀ y ∈ A, dist x y ≠ 1
    55→
    56→/--
    57→Erdős Problem 232 [Er85, p.4] (PROVED):
    58→
    59→For every measurable unit-distance-free set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$, the upper density
    60→$$\limsup_{R\to\infty} \frac{\lambda(A \cap B_R)}{\lambda(B_R)} \le \frac{1}{4}.$$
    61→
    62→Formalized as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, for all sufficiently large $R$, the density
    63→ratio $\lambda(A \cap B_R) / \lambda(B_R) \le 1/4 + \varepsilon$. This is equivalent to the
    64→$\limsup$ being at most $1/4$.
    65→
    66→Proved by Ambrus–Csiszárik–Matolcsi–Varga–Zsámboki [ACMVZ23] who showed
    67→the stronger bound $m_1 \le 0.247$.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
    70→theorem erdos_232 : answer(True) ↔
    71→    ∀ (A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    72→      MeasurableSet A → IsUnitDistanceFree A →
    73→      ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    74→        ∃ R₀ : ℝ, 0 < R₀ ∧ ∀ R : ℝ, R₀ ≤ R →
    75→          (volume (A ∩ Metric.ball (0 : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) R)).toReal /
    76→          (volume (Metric.ball (0 : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) R)).toReal ≤ 1 / 4 + ε := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→end Erdos232
    80→

    20→# Erdős Problem 1126
    21→
    22→If `f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y)` for almost all `x, y ∈ ℝ`, then there exists an additive function `g`
    23→such that `f = g` almost everywhere.
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1126](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1126)
    26→
    27→[dB66] de Bruijn, N.G., _A difference property for Riemann integrable functions and for
    28→some similar classes of functions_. Indag. Math. 28 (1966), 145-151.
    29→
    30→[Ju65] Jurkat, W.B., _On Cauchy's functional equation_. Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 16 (1965),
    31→683-686.

    40→
    41→If $f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y)$ for almost all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ then there exists a function $g$
    42→such that $g(x+y) = g(x) + g(y)$ for all $x, y \in \mathbb{R}$ and $f(x) = g(x)$ for almost
    43→all $x$.
    44→-/
    45→@[category research solved, AMS 28 39]
    46→theorem erdos_1126 (f : ℝ → ℝ)
    47→    (hf : ∀ᵐ p : ℝ × ℝ ∂(volume.prod volume), f (p.1 + p.2) = f p.1 + f p.2) :
    48→    ∃ g : ℝ → ℝ,
    49→      (∀ x y : ℝ, g (x + y) = g x + g y) ∧
    50→      (∀ᵐ x ∂volume, f x = g x) := by
    51→  sorry
    52→
    53→end Erdos1126

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 769
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/769](https://www.erdosproblems.com/769)
    23→
    24→Let $c(n)$ be minimal such that if $k \geq c(n)$ then the $n$-dimensional unit cube
    25→can be decomposed into $k$ homothetic $n$-dimensional cubes. Is it true that
    26→$c(n) \gg n^n$?
    27→
    28→A problem first investigated by Hadwiger, who proved $c(n) \geq 2^n + 2^{n-1}$.
    29→
    30→[Er74b] Burgess, D.A. and Erdős, P., on a problem in combinatorial geometry, 1974.
    31→
    32→[CoMa18] Connor, S. and Marmorino, M., on cube decompositions, 2018.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→namespace Erdos769
    36→
    37→/-- The unit cube $[0,1]^n$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$. -/
    38→def unitCube (n : ℕ) : Set (Fin n → ℝ) :=
    39→  {x | ∀ i, 0 ≤ x i ∧ x i ≤ 1}
    40→
    41→/-- An axis-aligned cube in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with given corner and side length. -/
    42→def axisCube {n : ℕ} (corner : Fin n → ℝ) (s : ℝ) : Set (Fin n → ℝ) :=
    43→  {x | ∀ i, corner i ≤ x i ∧ x i ≤ corner i + s}
    44→
    45→/-- The interior of an axis-aligned cube in $\mathbb{R}^n$. -/
    46→def axisCubeInterior {n : ℕ} (corner : Fin n → ℝ) (s : ℝ) : Set (Fin n → ℝ) :=
    47→  {x | ∀ i, corner i < x i ∧ x i < corner i + s}
    48→
    49→/-- The $n$-dimensional unit cube $[0,1]^n$ can be decomposed into exactly $k$
    50→homothetic cubes (axis-aligned cubes with positive side lengths) such that
    51→the cubes cover the unit cube and have pairwise disjoint interiors. -/
    52→def CanDecomposeUnitCube (n k : ℕ) : Prop :=
    53→  ∃ (corners : Fin k → Fin n → ℝ) (sides : Fin k → ℝ),
    54→    (∀ j, sides j > 0) ∧
    55→    (∀ j, axisCube (corners j) (sides j) ⊆ unitCube n) ∧
    56→    (∀ x ∈ unitCube n, ∃ j, x ∈ axisCube (corners j) (sides j)) ∧
    57→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j → ∀ x,
    58→      ¬(x ∈ axisCubeInterior (corners i) (sides i) ∧
    59→        x ∈ axisCubeInterior (corners j) (sides j)))
    60→
    61→/--
    62→Erdős Problem 769 [Er74b]:
    63→
    64→Let $c(n)$ be minimal such that if $k \geq c(n)$ then the $n$-dimensional unit cube
    65→can be decomposed into $k$ homothetic $n$-dimensional cubes. Is it true that
    66→$c(n) \gg n^n$?
    67→
    68→Formalized as: there exists $C > 0$ and $N_0$ such that for all $n \geq N_0$,
    69→if every $k \geq m$ permits a decomposition of the unit $n$-cube into $k$ homothetic
    70→cubes, then $m \geq C \cdot n^n$ (i.e., the threshold $c(n) \geq C \cdot n^n$).
    71→-/
    72→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    73→theorem erdos_769 : answer(sorry) ↔
    74→    ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧ ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N₀ →
    75→      ∀ m : ℕ, (∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ m → CanDecomposeUnitCube n k) →
    76→        (m : ℝ) ≥ C * (n : ℝ) ^ n := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→end Erdos769
    80→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 743
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/743](https://www.erdosproblems.com/743)
    23→
    24→Let $T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be a collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices.
    25→Can we always write $K_n$ as the edge disjoint union of the $T_k$?
    26→
    27→A conjecture of Gyárfás, known as the tree packing conjecture.
    28→
    29→Gyárfás and Lehel proved that this holds if all but at most 2 of the trees are
    30→stars, or if all the trees are stars or paths. Fishburn proved this for $n \leq 9$.
    31→Bollobás proved that the smallest $\lfloor n/\sqrt{2} \rfloor$ many trees can always be packed
    32→greedily into $K_n$.
    33→
    34→Joos, Kim, Kühn, and Osthus proved this conjecture holds when the trees
    35→have bounded maximum degree. Janzer and Montgomery proved that there exists
    36→some $c > 0$ such that the largest $cn$ trees can be packed into $K_n$.
    37→
    38→[Er81] Erdős, P.
    39→-/
    40→
    41→open SimpleGraph
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos743
    44→
    45→/--
    46→**Erdős Problem 743** [Er81]:
    47→
    48→Let $T_2, \ldots, T_n$ be any collection of trees such that $T_k$ has $k$ vertices.
    49→Then the complete graph $K_n$ can be decomposed into edge-disjoint copies
    50→of these trees. Also known as the tree packing conjecture (Gyárfás).
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    53→theorem erdos_743 : answer(sorry) ↔
    54→    ∀ (n : ℕ), 2 ≤ n →
    55→    ∀ (T : (k : Fin (n - 1)) → SimpleGraph (Fin (k.val + 2))),
    56→    (∀ k, (T k).IsTree) →
    57→    ∃ (f : (k : Fin (n - 1)) → Fin (k.val + 2) → Fin n),
    58→      -- Each embedding is injective
    59→      (∀ k, Function.Injective (f k)) ∧
    60→      -- The edge images are pairwise disjoint across different trees
    61→      (∀ k₁ k₂, k₁ ≠ k₂ →
    62→        ∀ a₁ b₁, (T k₁).Adj a₁ b₁ →
    63→        ∀ a₂ b₂, (T k₂).Adj a₂ b₂ →
    64→        Sym2.mk (f k₁ a₁, f k₁ b₁) ≠ Sym2.mk (f k₂ a₂, f k₂ b₂)) ∧
    65→      -- Every edge of Kₙ is covered
    66→      (∀ v w : Fin n, v ≠ w →
    67→        ∃ k a b, (T k).Adj a b ∧
    68→          Sym2.mk (v, w) = Sym2.mk (f k a, f k b)) := by
    69→  sorry
    70→
    71→end Erdos743
    72→
